content.text: few things, i have zellij installed, but it doesn't seem to have permanence and other tmux things i'd expect from a remote dev env. seems like pi just starts as a fresh process or at least session every time i connect to zellij, also resizing text is still quite weird in zellij, it seems to resize the entire zellij window instead of text inside a pane

Issue split:

```
SSH/terminal
  -> zellij server/session
    -> panes/processes
      -> pi process
```

### 1. Zellij permanence

Zellij is persistent only if you reattach same named session.

Use:

```bash
zellij ls
zellij attach <name>
```

Better remote-dev entrypoint:

```bash
zellij attach dev || zellij --session dev
```

If you just run `zellij`, it creates a new anonymous/fresh session.

For stronger restore after reboot/crash, enable Zellij session serialization/resurrection. But that usually restores layout/commands, not arbitrary process state like a live `pi` conversation unless the process itself persists.

### 2. Pi appears fresh

Likely cause:

```
new zellij session -> new shell -> new pi process -> fresh pi runtime
```

Fix:

```bash
zellij attach dev || zellij --session dev
# start pi once inside it
```

Then detach with:

```text
Ctrl-p d
```

Do not exit shell/pi if you want it kept.

### 3. Text resizing

Zellij does not control font size inside panes. Font size is owned by your terminal emulator.

So if zoom behaves weird:

```
terminal font zoom -> whole terminal grid changes
zellij reacts -> panes resize/reflow
```

Expected. Zellij can resize panes, not text independently per pane.

Use terminal emulator font controls instead:
- <REDACTED_BLOB>: font zoom changes whole terminal.
- Per-pane font size is generally not a terminal multiplexer feature.

### Next step

Set shell alias on the remote host:

```bash
alias zd='zellij attach dev || zellij --session dev'
```

Then always enter with `zd`.

Yes, that’s not Zellij pane layout. It’s terminal viewport/grid mismatch.

From screenshot:

```
window physical size: huge
terminal PTY grid: smaller
zellij draws only to reported rows/cols
unused area: black below/right
```

Likely cause: terminal emulator zoomed font down, but SSH/Zellij didn’t get a correct resize event / PTY size update.

Fix attempts, in order:

### 1. Force terminal resize update

Inside zellij:

```bash
stty size
```

Then physically resize the terminal window a little. Check:

```bash
stty size
```

Rows/cols should change.

### 2. Detach/reattach after zoom

```text
Ctrl-p d
```

Then:

```bash
zellij attach main
```

This often re-negotiates terminal size.

### 3. Restart zellij client, not session

Exit only the attached client. Reattach same session.

```bash
zellij ls
zellij attach main
```

### 4. Suspect terminal app / SSH wrapper

Your left sidebar says “Remote proxy to devbox unavailable”, so this may be Cursor/VS Code remote terminal behavior, not Zellij.

Cause chain:

```
font zoom in GUI terminal
  -> terminal fails to update PTY rows/cols
  -> ssh/zellij sees old grid
  -> panes don't fill visual window
```

Quick verify: open a normal macOS Terminal/iTerm/Ghostty SSH session, attach same zellij session. If it fills correctly, Cursor terminal is the issue.

Next step: try detach/reattach after zoom. Then test same session from a normal terminal.

```
font zoom out
  -> visual window grows grid capacity

  -> remote zellij keeps old grid
```

Use as workaround:

- For serious Zellij work: use Ghostty/iTerm2/Terminal.app SSH into host.
- In cmux/Cursor: avoid font zoom after Zellij starts.
- If zooming: detach/reattach or restart terminal tab.

Then resize/zoom and re-run:

If rows/cols don’t change correctly, terminal wrapper bug confirmed.

```bash
ssh devbox -t 'zellij attach main || zellij --session main'
```

cmux terminal rendering uses **Ghostty config**, not only cmux UI config.

Config locations:

```text
~/.config/ghostty/config
~/Library/Application Support/com.mitchellh.ghostty/config
~/.config/cmux/cmux.json
```

Set terminal font size in Ghostty config:

```conf
font-family = SF Mono
font-size = 13
```

cmux-specific reload:

```bash
cmux reload-config
```

or `Cmd+Shift+,`.

Important distinction:

```text
Ghostty font-size       -> terminal cell/font size
cmux/browser zoom       -> app surface zoom, may break PTY sizing
Zellij resize           -> pane dimensions only
```

Your screenshot looks like you used **cmux/browser zoom out** (`Cmd+-`) rather than terminal font sizing. cmux docs list:

Yes-ish. Expected from **browser/app zoom**, not expected from a proper terminal font-size change.

cmux has two different “zoom” concepts:

```
Cmd+- / Cmd+= / Cmd+0
  -> browser/app zoom
  -> scales the whole UI surface
  -> can desync terminal grid vs visible area
  -> black unused space possible

Ghostty font-size config
  -> actual terminal font/cell size
  -> terminal recalculates rows/cols
  -> Zellij should fill space
```

So behavior is “explainable”, but bad UX for terminal work.

If you specifically want boring reliable remote persistence:

```bash
tmux new -A -s main
```

cmux reads terminal configuration from Ghostty config files. cmux-owned app settings also live in ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json, including shortcuts, automation, sidebar behavior, notifications, and browser preferences.

## [#](#config-locations)Config file locations

cmux looks for configuration in these locations (in order):

1.  `~/.config/ghostty/config`
2.  `~/Library/Application Support/com.mitchellh.ghostty/config`

Create the config file if it doesn't exist:

```
mkdir -p ~/.config/ghostty
touch ~/.config/ghostty/config
```

## [#](#example-config)Example config

~/.config/ghostty/config

```
font-family = SF Mono
font-size = 13
sidebar-font-size = 14
surface-tab-bar-font-size = 11
theme = One Dark
scrollback-limit = 50000000
split-divider-color = #3e4451
working-directory = ~/code
```

cmux keeps app-owned settings, shortcuts, actions, custom commands, and workspace layouts in `~/.config/cmux/cmux.json`. Terminal rendering still lives in Ghostty config. On launch, if the file is missing, cmux writes a commented template there.

Open cmux Settings, then use the `cmux.json` section to open the canonical file in your preferred text editor.

1.  `~/.config/cmux/cmux.json`
2.  `.cmux/cmux.json` in a project for project-scoped actions and workspace commands

**Precedence:** global `~/.config/cmux/cmux.json` settings override values saved in the Settings window. Legacy `~/.config/cmux/settings.json` and Application Support settings files are read only as fallback for missing settings keys. Project-local `.cmux/cmux.json` can override actions, commands, UI action wiring, and notification hooks, but not global app preferences.

**Reload:** edit the file, then use `Cmd+Shift+,` or

`cmux reload-config` to re-read it without restarting the app.

**Migrations:** keep `schemaVersion` at `1` for now. Future cmux versions will use that field for upgrades. If cmux sees a newer schema version, it logs a warning and parses known keys only.

The file accepts JSON with comments and trailing commas. The canonical schema is published at [https://raw.githubusercontent.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/main/web/data/cmux.schema.json](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/main/web/data/cmux.schema.json) and the source lives at [https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/blob/main/web/data/cmux.schema.json](https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux/blob/main/web/data/cmux.schema.json).

Yes. Your read is right.

Nested multiplexers conflict:

```
cmux
  tabs / panes / remote restore / agent awareness
    ↓
zellij
  tabs / panes / session lifecycle / keybindings
    ↓
pi
```

Result:

- cmux panes become just “zellij client holders”
- zellij owns actual workspace state
- cmux restore can create extra zellij clients
- stale clients keep odd PTY sizes
- two tab systems, two pane systems, two persistence models
- phone access needs attach logic anyway

So don’t use Zellij as default inside cmux.

What you want:

```
durable project terminals
same pi session
resume from laptop/phone
minimal UI fighting
```

Best model:

### Primary: cmux owns desktop workflow

```
cmux workspace = project
cmux pane/tab = long-running terminal/agent
pi runs directly in pane
```

No zellij. No tmux. Let cmux do its thing.

### Secondary: tmux only for non-cmux access

For phone/plain SSH:

```bash
tmux new -A -s home-server
```

But don’t auto-run this inside every cmux pane. Use it only when outside cmux.

Recommended split:

```text
Mac desktop:
  cmux -> ssh devbox -> cd project -> pi

Phone:
  ssh devbox -> tmux new -A -s home-server
```

If you need the *exact same* terminal from both cmux and phone, then cmux alone cannot provide that to arbitrary SSH clients. You need tmux/zellij/screen as the shared backend.

But then accept:

```
shared terminal backend owns panes/session
cmux becomes just a viewer
```

Decision:

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Best cmux UX | no zellij/tmux in cmux |
| Same terminal from phone + cmux | tmux, cmux is just transport |
| Fancy terminal workspace | zellij/tmux, not cmux panes |

My recommendation:

```
cmux for daily desktop
tmux for phone/emergency
do not nest zellij by default
```

Remove this:

```bash
alias dev='zellij attach --create main'
```

Replace with:

```bash
alias dev='cd ~/projects/home-server && pi'
alias tdev='tmux new -A -s home-server'
```

Use `tdev` only when you explicitly want shared terminal persistence.
is there a different protocol instead of tmux over ssh maybe there are other solutions?

Yes, but categories matter.

```
problem A: survive flaky network
problem B: keep process running after disconnect
problem C: attach same terminal from many clients
```

### Options

| Tool/protocol | Solves | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| SSH only | basic remote shell | process dies on disconnect unless app persists itself |
| Mosh | flaky/mobile networks | does **not** provide durable sessions by itself |
| Eternal Terminal (`et`) | reconnectable shell transport | better SSH-like persistence, less universal |
| tmux/screen | durable shared terminal | boring, reliable, works everywhere |
| Zellij | durable shared terminal + UI | nicer, but you hit nesting pain |
| tmate | shared tmux over relay | good for temporary sharing, not daily private dev |
| ttyd/wetty | browser terminal | usually wrap tmux underneath |
| VS Code/Cursor Remote | IDE remote protocol | good editor workflow, not universal terminal continuity |
| cmux remote | app-specific remote terminal/session manager | good inside cmux, not from phone/plain SSH unless supported |

Key caveat:

If you want:

```
same live terminal
from cmux + phone + random laptop
```

then some server-side session owner must exist:

```
tmux / screen / zellij / et / app-specific daemon
```

There is no magic SSH mode that does this cleanly.

Best practical setup:

### Desktop
Use cmux directly. No tmux/zellij.

### Phone / emergency
Use one tmux session per project:

```bash
tmux new -A -s home-server
```

### Flaky mobile SSH

# RPC Mode

RPC mode enables headless operation of the coding agent via a JSON protocol over stdin/stdout. This is useful for embedding the agent in other applications, IDEs, or custom UIs.

**Note for Node.js/TypeScript users**: If you're building a Node.js application, consider using `AgentSession` directly from `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent` instead of spawning a subprocess. See [`src/core/agent-session.ts`](../src/core/agent-session.ts) for the API. For a subprocess-based TypeScript client, see [`src/modes/rpc/rpc-client.ts`](../src/modes/rpc/rpc-client.ts).

## Starting RPC Mode

```bash
pi --mode rpc [options]
```

Common options:
- `--provider <name>`: Set the LLM provider (anthropic, openai, google, etc.)
- `--model <pattern>`: Model pattern or ID (supports `provider/id` and optional `:<thinking>`)
- `--name <name>` / `-n <name>`: Set the session display name at startup
- `--no-session`: Disable session persistence
- `--session-dir <path>`: Custom session storage directory

## Protocol Overview

- **Commands**: JSON objects sent to stdin, one per line
- **Responses**: JSON objects with `type: "response"` indicating command success/failure
- **Events**: Agent events streamed to stdout as JSON lines

All commands support an optional `id` field for request/response correlation. If provided, the corresponding response will include the same `id`.

### Framing

RPC mode uses strict JSONL semantics with LF (`\n`) as the only record delimiter.

This matters for clients:
- Split records on `\n` only
- Accept optional `\r\n` input by stripping a trailing `\r`
- Do not use generic line readers that treat Unicode separators as newlines

In particular, Node `readline` is not protocol-compliant for RPC mode because it also splits on `U+2028` and `U+2029`, which are valid inside JSON strings.

## Commands

### Prompting

#### prompt

Send a user prompt to the agent. The command response is emitted after the prompt is accepted, queued, or handled. Events continue streaming asynchronously after acceptance.

```json
{"id": "req-1", "type": "prompt", "message": "Hello, world!"}
```

With images:
```json
{"type": "prompt", "message": "What's in this image?", "images": [{"type": "image", "data": "base64-encoded-data", "mimeType": "image/png"}]}
```

**During streaming**: If the agent is already streaming, you must specify `streamingBehavior` to queue the message:

```json
{"type": "prompt", "message": "New instruction", "streamingBehavior": "steer"}
```

- `"steer"`: Queue the message while the agent is running. It is delivered after the current assistant turn finishes executing its tool calls, before the next LLM call.
- `"followUp"`: Wait until the agent finishes. Message is delivered only when agent stops.

If the agent is streaming and no `streamingBehavior` is specified, the command returns an error.

**Extension commands**: If the message is an extension command (e.g., `/mycommand`), it executes immediately even during streaming. Extension commands manage their own LLM interaction via `pi.sendMessage()`.

**Input expansion**: Skill commands (`/skill:name`) and prompt templates (`/template`) are expanded before sending/queueing.

Response:
```json
{"id": "req-1", "type": "response", "command": "prompt", "success": true}
```

`success: true` means the prompt was accepted, queued, or handled immediately. `success: false` means the prompt was rejected before acceptance. Failures after acceptance are reported through the normal event and message stream, not as a second `response` for the same request id.

The `images` field is optional. Each image uses `ImageContent` format: `{"type": "image", "data": "base64-encoded-data", "mimeType": "image/png"}`.

Yes. You may not need a full terminal.

Pi already has the right primitive:

```
pi --mode rpc
```

Meaning:

```
durable pi daemon/session
  <- mobile/web/chat client sends prompts
  -> streams events/results back
```

That avoids:

```
phone terminal
ssh
tmux/zellij
PTY sizing
terminal keybindings
```

Better target architecture:

```
devbox
  pi RPC service per project
    home-server -> cwd ~/projects/home-server
    other-proj  -> cwd ~/projects/other-proj

clients
  cmux terminal / desktop UI
  phone web app
  simple CLI "remote-control"
```

For mobile you want “talk to pi”, not “attach to terminal”.

Pi supports:
- SDK embedding
- RPC JSONL over stdin/stdout
- persistent sessions via session files
- session naming with `--name`
- custom `--session-dir`

Caveat: `pi --mode rpc` is stdin/stdout, not network server by itself. Need thin wrapper:

```
HTTP/WebSocket server
  -> owns long-running pi RPC child
  -> sends prompt JSONL
  -> streams events to clients
```

That is the clean version of Codex-mobile-style control.

Immediate implication:

- Keep cmux for desktop terminals.
- Remove zellij default.
- Build/use `remote-control` as a Pi RPC bridge.
- Phone talks to bridge, not terminal.

Can build minimal:
1. `systemd --user` service: one Pi RPC process per project.
2. local WebSocket/HTTP bridge.
3. phone web UI with prompt box + event stream.

talk to me first, what's your plan

Plan: make Zellij explicit and single-client-ish. Stop `dev` from blindly piling clients onto `main`.

Current bad behavior:

```bash
alias dev='zellij attach --create main'
```

Each run:

```
new cmux pane/surface
  -> dev
    -> attaches another client to same zellij session
      -> stale PTY sizes accumulate
```

### Desired flow

`dev` should become a small session chooser:

```
dev
  -> show existing zellij sessions
  -> choose:
      attach existing
      create named session
      kill stale session/client if needed
```

But Zellij has weak client management. It can list clients:

```bash
zellij action list-clients
```

It does not cleanly “detach client X” from outside, so cleanup is usually process-level or session-level.

### Proposed implementation

Replace alias with script:

```text
~/.local/bin/dev
```

Behavior:

1. If already inside Zellij:
   - print “already in zellij”
   - avoid nesting.

2. If no args:
   - list sessions:
     ```bash
     zellij list-sessions
     ```
   - if exactly one session, attach it.
   - if none, create session from current dir name.
   - if multiple, use `fzf` if available, else print names and ask.

3. If arg provided:
   ```bash
   dev home-server
   ```
   attaches/creates that named session:
   ```bash
   zellij attach --create home-server
   ```

4. Add helper:
   ```bash
   dev --ls
   dev --clients
   dev --kill home-server
   ```

5. Remove `.bashrc` alias.

### Naming strategy

Use project-specific sessions, not global `main`:

```text
home-server
pi-agent
notes
```

So:

```bash
dev home-server
```

Not:

```bash
dev -> main forever
```

### Cleanup now

Before switching:

```bash
zellij action list-clients
ps -ef | grep '[z]ellij attach'
kill <stale attach pids>
```

Or blunt:

```bash
zellij kill-session main
```

Warning: kills live panes/processes in that session.

My recommendation:
- implement `dev` script
- remove alias
- use `dev home-server`
- avoid cmux panes all auto-attaching to same session.
ok, maybe let's bring it back, what is the general approach to this problem? how do poeple set up remote dev environment with ai agents?

General pattern: separate **workspace persistence** from **agent conversation persistence**.

```
remote devbox
  code/repos
  secrets
  long-running shells
  agent session files
clients
  laptop IDE/terminal
  phone/chat UI
```

### Common setups

| Setup | How it works | Good | Bad |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plain SSH + tmux | tmux owns durable shells | boring, reliable, phone-friendly | ugly UX, nested with IDEs |
| IDE remote | Cursor/VS Code/JetBrains owns workspace | best desktop UX | mobile weak, terminal persistence varies |
| Web IDE | code-server/Coder/Gitpod | accessible anywhere | more infra, browser constraints |
| Agent-native sessions | Codex/Claude/Pi session files + resume | no terminal needed | each tool has own UX/protocol |
| Chat bridge to agent | web/mobile UI talks to agent daemon | best mobile | needs custom service/security |

### What people usually do

#### 1. Desktop-first developers

```
Mac/Windows
  -> Cursor/VS Code Remote SSH
    -> devbox
      -> agents run in IDE terminal
```

Persistence comes from:
- IDE reconnect
- agent resume command
- Git as durable state

Not usually exact same live terminal.

#### 2. Terminal-first developers

```
laptop/phone
  -> mosh/ssh
    -> tmux
      -> nvim + agent CLIs
```

Persistence comes from tmux. Simple and robust.

#### 3. AI-agent-heavy workflow

```
devbox
  -> one agent session per project/task
  -> session logs/files persist
client
  -> attach/resume by session id
```

They care less about terminal continuity and more about:
- repo state
- conversation/session resume
- task logs
- notifications

#### 4. Mobile control

Usually not full terminal. More like:

```
phone
  -> web/chat app
    -> agent daemon/API
      -> repo tools
```

Codex-style. Terminal is fallback.

### Key design decision

Do you want exact same live shell?

```
yes -> tmux/zellij/screen required
no  -> use agent/session resume + cmux/IDE terminals
```

Exact live shell is expensive:
- PTY sizing
- keybindings
- nested panes
- stale clients
- mobile terminal pain

Agent resume is cleaner:
- start new UI anywhere
- resume same conversation/task
- no terminal emulation dependency

### Best setup for you

Given cmux + pi:

```
Desktop:
  cmux workspace per project
  pi directly in cmux terminal
  no zellij by default

Remote/mobile:
  pi session resume or RPC/chat bridge
  tmux only as emergency fallback
```

Mental model:

```
cmux = desktop workspace manager
pi sessions = durable AI memory
git = durable code state
tmux = emergency shared terminal
```

Avoid:

```
cmux panes
  -> zellij panes
    -> pi panes/session
```

That stacks three workspace managers.

```
PC/Mac client
  -> cmux/terminal UI

Phone client

```

The phone needs:

- current task status
- latest assistant output
- pending question/approval
- prompt box: “steer/follow-up”
- stop button
- maybe diff/test summary

### Why terminal sharing feels bad

Terminal sharing optimizes for:

```
same shell, same panes, same scrollback
```

But your mobile use case is:

```
agent still running?
what changed?
send correction
approve/deny
```

That is agent-control, not terminal-control.

### Practical short-term setup

Use cmux on PC/Mac. No Zellij default.

For phone fallback:

```bash
tmux new -A -s home-server
```

Only for emergency terminal access.

For real solution later:

```text
pi RPC bridge
  -> web UI / mobile PWA
```

Pi has `--mode rpc`, so this is feasible.

### Decision

Use two layers:

```
desktop work = cmux terminal
mobile supervision = pi remote-control
emergency access = tmux
```

Do not make Zellij the center. It solves the wrong problem for your phone use case.

# Session Handoff

## Repository

Project path: `/Users/syzom/projects/home-server`.

Current branch: `main`.

This repository manages a live Proxmox/NixOS home server.

## Current Objective

Complete v1 acceptance for the deployed `core` VM: Tailscale verification,
restic backup/restore proof, LAN-only admin UI exposure, and DNS handoff
decision.

## Accepted Current Shape

V1 is intentionally narrow:

```text
mini PC
└── Proxmox VE bootstrap boundary
    └── VM: core
        ├── NixOS
        ├── Tailscale
        ├── DNS blocker
        ├── restic backups to Cloudflare R2
        └── simple local status monitoring
```

V1 excludes GitHub runner, staging apps, long-running agent host, Home Assistant, Nextcloud, Immich, dashboard, and external host-down alerting.

## Hardware

Known mini PC specs:

- CPU: AMD Ryzen 7 PRO 8845HS.
- Memory: 24 GB RAM.
- Storage: 1 TB SSD.

Capacity supports Proxmox plus conservative VMs. Complexity, not hardware, is the remaining concern.

## Durable Docs To Read First

1. `AGENTS.md` for the project map.
2. `docs-ai/docs/architecture-decision-v1.md` for Proxmox/NixOS reasoning.
3. `docs-ai/docs/hardware-profile.md` for capacity assumptions.
4. `docs-ai/docs/backup-strategy.md` for R2/restic direction.
5. `docs-ai/docs/workload-candidates.md` for accepted, deferred, and out-of-scope services.
6. `docs-ai/docs/security-and-secrets.md` for SOPS/age and runner risk.
7. `docs-ai/docs/validation-strategy.md` for proof expectations.
8. `docs-ai/current-work/initial-planning.md` for active decisions.
9. `docs-ai/docs/devbox-design.md` for the current V2 devbox shape.

## Current Decisions

- Proxmox is selected because it gives rollback and a clean path to future isolated VMs.
- NixOS is selected for the `core` VM because code-owned system state is a primary objective.
- Cloudflare R2 is the preferred off-machine backup target.
- R2 infrastructure should be managed by IaC, not only dashboard clicks.
- restic is the preferred first backup tool unless research finds a blocker.
- Monitoring should be simple and local in V1; external host-down alerting is deferred.
- GitHub runner is V2 and must include isolation plus cache lifecycle design.
- Databases for staging apps are per-service, not a shared platform.
- Hosted Bitwarden stays; do not self-host password management for V1.
- Existing Syncthing stays; Nextcloud is deferred.
- Jellyfin/media work is out of scope and belongs to a separate movie-server project.

None for core v1.

# Terminal Setup

Pi uses the [Kitty keyboard protocol](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/keyboard-protocol/) for reliable modifier key detection. Most modern terminals support this protocol, but some require configuration.

## Kitty, iTerm2

Work out of the box.

## Apple Terminal

Pi enables enhanced key reporting when available. If Terminal.app still sends plain Return for `Shift+Enter`, pi uses a local macOS modifier fallback to treat that Return as `Shift+Enter`.

This fallback only works when pi runs on the same Mac as Terminal.app. It cannot detect the local keyboard over remote SSH.

## Ghostty

Add to your Ghostty config (`~/Library/Application Support/com.mitchellh.ghostty/config` on macOS, `~/.config/ghostty/config` on Linux):

```
keybind = alt+backspace=text:\x1b\x7f
```

Older Claude Code versions may have added this Ghostty mapping:

```
keybind = shift+enter=text:\n
```

That mapping sends a raw linefeed byte. Inside pi, that is indistinguishable from `Ctrl+J`, so tmux and pi no longer see a real `shift+enter` key event.

If Claude Code 2.x or newer is the only reason you added that mapping, you can remove it, unless you want to use Claude Code in tmux, where it still requires that Ghostty mapping.

If you want `Shift+Enter` to keep working in tmux via that remap, add `ctrl+j` to your pi `newLine` keybinding in `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`:

```json
{
  "newLine": ["shift+enter", "ctrl+j"]
}
```

## WezTerm

Create `~/.wezterm.lua`:

```lua
local wezterm = require 'wezterm'
local config = wezterm.config_builder()
config.enable_kitty_keyboard = true
return config
```

On WSL, WezTerm may require a visible hardware cursor for IME candidate window positioning. If CJK IME candidates do not follow the text cursor, set `PI_HARDWARE_CURSOR=1` before running pi or set `showHardwareCursor` to `true` in settings.

## VS Code (Integrated Terminal)

`keybindings.json` locations:
- macOS: `~/Library/Application Support/Code/User/keybindings.json`
- Linux: `~/.config/Code/User/keybindings.json`
- Windows: `%APPDATA%\\Code\\User\\keybindings.json`

Add to `keybindings.json` to enable `Shift+Enter` for multi-line input:

```json
{
  "key": "shift+enter",
  "command": "workbench.action.terminal.sendSequence",
  "args": { "text": "\u001b[13;2u" },
  "when": "terminalFocus"
}
```

## Windows Terminal

Add to `settings.json` (Ctrl+Shift+, or Settings → Open JSON file) to forward the modified Enter keys pi uses:

```json
{
  "actions": [
    {
      "command": { "action": "sendInput", "input": "\u001b[13;2u" },
      "keys": "shift+enter"
    },
    {
      "command": { "action": "sendInput", "input": "\u001b[13;3u" },
      "keys": "alt+enter"
    }
  ]
}
```

- `Shift+Enter` inserts a new line.
- Windows Terminal binds `Alt+Enter` to fullscreen by default. That prevents pi from receiving `Alt+Enter` for follow-up queueing.
- Remapping `Alt+Enter` to `sendInput` forwards the real key chord to pi instead.

If you already have an `actions` array, add the objects to it. If the old fullscreen behavior persists, fully close and reopen Windows Terminal.

## xfce4-terminal, terminator

These terminals have limited escape sequence support. Modified Enter keys like `Ctrl+Enter` and `Shift+Enter` cannot be distinguished from plain `Enter`, preventing custom keybindings such as `submit: ["ctrl+enter"]` from working.

For the best experience, use a terminal that supports the Kitty keyboard protocol:
- [Kitty](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/)
- [Ghostty](https://ghostty.org/)
- [WezTerm](https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/)
- [iTerm2](https://iterm2.com/)
- [Alacritty](https://github.com/alacritty/alacritty) (requires compilation with Kitty protocol support)

## IntelliJ IDEA (Integrated Terminal)

The built-in terminal has limited escape sequence support. Shift+Enter cannot be distinguished from Enter in IntelliJ's terminal.

If you want the hardware cursor visible, set `PI_HARDWARE_CURSOR=1` before running pi (disabled by default for compatibility).

Consider using a dedicated terminal emulator for the best experience.

Not TERM. Not pi. Not Zellij.

cmux local UI says current terminal view is full size:

```text
hosted_view_frame: 2344x1288 px
surface: 7A1E5456...
```

But cmux remote PTY attachment says current remote terminal is tiny:

```text
attachment_id: 7A1E5456...
rows: 71
cols: 72
```

Kernel PTY agrees:

```text
/dev/pts/14 -> 71 72
pi pid 124273 -> pts/14
```

So pi is drawing correctly into the PTY size it was given. cmux remote failed to propagate actual view size to the remote PTY.

Extra evidence: same workspace has old unattached PTY sessions with sane sizes:

```text
75x292
75x145
71x72  <- current attached one
```

So cmux remote session state is accumulating stale PTYs/attachments and current surface is attached to the wrong/small geometry.

I tried cmux RPC:

```text
surface.refresh -> no resize
workspace.equalize_splits -> no-op
workspace.remote.pty_resize -> requires internal attachment_token
```

So external repair is blocked by cmux internals.

Current root cause:

```text
cmux remote terminal portal has full UI bounds
but remote PTY attachment remains 72x71
=> cmux remote resize/attachment bug
```

Safe fix options:

1. Close/recreate this cmux remote terminal surface.
   - Kills current live pi process unless pi session is resumable.
2. Disconnect/reconnect cmux remote workspace.
3. Report upstream with this exact evidence.

I would also remove the TERM override. It is unrelated noise.
